dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 responding for unconscious/fainting/near-fainting at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Jordan, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing and chest pain since I woke up from a faint three hours ago.
Mr. Smith, can you open your eyes and tell me your name?
James… James Smith.
Good. What day is it today?
Wednesday, I think.
Okay, if I tap your shoulder, can you point to it?
Yep, right here. Where'd you tap?
Mr. Smith, we're going to look at your airway now. Can you lift your chin up a little for me?
Sure.
Respirations are 30 per minute. The patient is tachypneic and using accessory muscles. I observed intercostal retractions.
There are signs of tripoding and increased work of breathing.
Radial pulses are strong, equal, and regular bilaterally. Skin is warm, dry, and has normal color with capillary nail refill less than two seconds.
Pulse is 112 at the moment.
Respiration rate is 30 per minute.
Blood pressure is 168 over 88.
The EKG here shows sinus tachycardia.
Oxygen saturation is 88% on room air.
Blood glucose is 114 mg/dL.
Mr. Smith, can you tell us more about your chest pain-where is it, and what does it feel like?
It's in my upper left chest-sharp, 10 out of 10, and it gets worse on deep breathing.
And how long as the pain been there? Is there anything that eases the pain at all?
It's been constant since the fainting incident. Staying still helps a little, but only a little.
Can you describe exactly what happened during the fainting incident three hours ago?
I just fainted, hit the floor hard, then woke up and realized I may have broken some ribs.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your chest pain right now?
It's 10 right now.
Has the pain spread to your neck, arms, or back at all?
No, it just stays in my chest.
Skin is dry, warm, and pink with no rashes, discoloration, or bruises.
HEENT normal-eyes are PERRL at 3mm with no discharge or swelling.
JVD not present. Scars and lymph nodes are within normal limits.
Neck, thoracic, and lumbar spine show no tenderness, deformities, or abnormalities.
We are about to listen to your lungs. Take a deep breath in for me.
Bilateral wheezing on expiration and inspiration, and decreased breath sounds-consistently in both lung fields.
No deformities or rib flail seen on visual inspection. The left rib cage is tender but has no open wounds.
The abdomen is soft, with normal bowel sounds and no tenderness or distension.
Gait normal prior to incident with full movement and strength in both arms and legs.
Pulses, motor, and sensory function are present bilaterally and symmetrically in all extremities.
Left rib area shows a small contusion and potential flail segment, but no open injury even with palpation.
You're using a lot of intercostal muscles for breathing-classic signs of increased work of breathing due to COPD or airway issues.
No crepitus, edema, or signs of puncture or laceration on the left rib cage.
No bruising, discoloration, or abrasions noted over the chest or extremities.
The 12-lead EKG shows ST depression in lead V5 and V6-possible subendocardial injury.
There is minimal sign of left ventricular hypertrophy visible in lead V5.
Some QRS widening detected suggesting a conduction issue.
Mr. Smith, with your consent, we will begin your treatment now.
Sure, whatever it takes to get me breathing easier.
We're starting four-lead ECG monitoring. You may not feel anything, but it's essential for evaluating your rhythm.
Power on at 16:30.
Next, I'm inserting an IV in your left arm at the antecubital vein using a 20G catheter for potential medication administration.
We are giving you a nebulizer treatment with Albuterol and Ipratropium-commonly known as a Duoneb.
This will usually take 10-15 minutes to help open up your airways. Any side effects like lightheadedness, let us know.
Your current pulse is 96.
Your respiratory rate improved to 21 per minute.
Blood pressure is still 168 over 98.
Oxygen saturation came up to 99% with 3LPM on nasal cannula.
EKG now shows a stable sinus rhythm.
Blood glucose is currently 0. This might be due to perfusion affecting the reading-let's keep this in mind.
Mr. Smith's mental status is alert and oriented with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 15.
We're following COPD exacerbation protocol as per your response and symptoms.
We are using Protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress and COPD management.
Beginning nasal capnography-moderate sharkfin waveform recorded showing effective oxygen delivery.
Your pulse is now 90, respirations 27, and your blood pressure is 148 over 92.
Oxygen saturation is at 100% with EKG still in a sinus rhythm.
Giving a second nebulizer treatment now-this one with 8LPM for higher oxygen support during nebulization.
Injecting IV Dexamethasone now for COPD flare management.
Mr. Smith, with the second medication, how is the pain or tightness in your chest as we speak?
It's still there, but the breathing does feel better.
I noticed you previously mentioned calling the Sotolol medicine a possible cause of the syncopal episodes. Is that still accurate?
Yes. It started causing fainting after they added it to my regimen when I was in the hospital.
Second Albuterol and Ipratropium given as per standing order under COPD protocol.
Dexamethasone is also given per COPD flare-up protocol.
With the second medication, how are you feeling now, Mr. Smith?
A bit better, though the tightness lingers-nothing major.
We are bringing you to [COMPANY_NAME]. They've been given a pre-arrival report with your condition.
That's where I was just in for the AICD firing and had been expected to be better.
We're moving you now to the stretcher-ready to be placed in Fowler's position for your breathing.
Mr. Smith is already secured onto the stretcher with shoulder and lap belts to allow safe transport.
We also have nasal capnography, EKG, and O2 running continuously en route.
No signs of injury related to sedative, hypnotic, or anxiolytic abuse are noted during body assessment.
No history or signs pointing to reactive airway or asthma, except for typical COPD effects.
Per discharge, you're on Pantoprazole which suggests GERD-no active gastrointestinal discomfort noted.
You're also on Rosuvastatin for cholesterol control-your skin and nails appear normal without signs of lipid-related issues.
Noting the ST depression and minimal LVH on 12-lead EKG may indicate possible subendocardial injury.
Per-standing order, all administered medications and procedures were given after IV access was successful.
No signs of croup. Chief complaint and physical character of the airway point to COPD, not pediatric-like croup symptoms.
No signs of lower extremity or pedal edema on visual examination of legs.
Remaining a/ox4; nothing neurological or neurocognitive involved that would affect mobility or coordination.
The doubling of your Albuterol and Ipratropium nebulizer and Dexamethasone shows new treatment for COPD flare.
You are using accessory muscles, tripod positioning, and intercostal retractions-consistent with significant work of breathing.
All interventions and medications were administered under standing order for respiratory distress.
After hospital confirmation, destination is [CITY], reiterating pre-arrival report of COPD flare and syncopal episode.
Fowler's position maintained with shoulder lap belts for transport and safety.
Per standing order, monitoring and reassessment of vitals and intervention response are occurring every 5 minutes.
Standing order medications are documented as given, and we are ready for hospital staff at the destination.
